coredata.dc:title: Assessing the electrocatalytic activity of a localized push-pull system in cobalt phthalocyanine/graphene quantum dot hybrids
coredata.dc:creator: Nkhahle, Reitumetse
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: In this study, two cobalt phthalocyanines (asymmetric complex 1) and symmetric (complex 2) were synthesized and conjugated to nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) through covalent and non-covalent means to create Co phthalocyanine (CoPc)-NGQDs hybrid systems. The CoPcs and conjugates were applied as electrode modifiers on a glassy carbon electrode in the electrochemical sensing of nitrite. Of the CoPcs alone, complex 1 performed better than complex 2 regarding the limits of detection (LoD) recorded (5.74 μM for 1 and 15.1 μM for 2). Regarding the conjugates/nanocomposites, the π-π stacked conjugate derived from complex 2 (2πNGQDs) demonstrated highly favourable electrochemical potential with an LoD value of 0.70 μM. The nanocomposites fashioned from complex 1 were marred by a reduced loading which rendered the conjugates poor electrochemical sensors. These observations however do not disqualify GQDs as complementary nanomaterials to phthalocyanines but rather shed light on seeking alternative routes to increasing the Pc loading in conjugates (more so in π-π stacked conjugates).

Acetaminophen

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The ease with which Pcs and NGQDs were merged into singular nanocomposites through ?-? stacking was facilitated by the structural makeup of the NGQDs which consists of planar sheets dominated by a network of sp2 carbons (Scheme 2A). Complex 1 was also linked to the NGQDs through a covalent bond (an amide bond). Using high resolution XPS spectra for C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, the presence of ?NH?, -COOH, and -OH groups on the surface of NGQDs has been established [23], hence their representation in Scheme 2 (See also Scheme S1 in Supporting Information).This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University. RN thanks NRF-DAAD for PhD scholarship funding.

head.ce:title: Assessing the electrocatalytic activity of a localized push-pull system in cobalt phthalocyanine/graphene quantum dot hybrids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6139, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6139, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, two cobalt phthalocyanines (asymmetric complex 1) and symmetric (complex 2) were synthesized and conjugated to nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) through covalent and non-covalent means to create Co phthalocyanine (CoPc)-NGQDs hybrid systems. The CoPcs and conjugates were applied as electrode modifiers on a glassy carbon electrode in the electrochemical sensing of nitrite. Of the CoPcs alone, complex 1 performed better than complex 2 regarding the limits of detection (LoD) recorded (5.74 μM for 1 and 15.1 μM for 2). Regarding the conjugates/nanocomposites, the π-π stacked conjugate derived from complex 2 (2πNGQDs) demonstrated highly favourable electrochemical potential with an LoD value of 0.70 μM. The nanocomposites fashioned from complex 1 were marred by a reduced loading which rendered the conjugates poor electrochemical sensors. These observations however do not disqualify GQDs as complementary nanomaterials to phthalocyanines but rather shed light on seeking alternative routes to increasing the Pc loading in conjugates (more so in π-π stacked conjugates).

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots are carbon-based nanomaterials fashioned from a network of five, six and/or seven membered carbon rings predominantly mimicking the appearance of a honeycomb [1,2]. The highly conjugated π-system gives rise to a multitude of electro-optic properties that are often improved by the inclusion of heteroatoms within the graphene matrix [2–4]. The addition of heteroatoms, which is often referred to as doping, facilitates the crossover from one field of application to another [3,4]. While doping has been established to improve the behaviour of GQDs, the combination of GQDs with biological entities and other organic and inorganic structures such as phthalocyanines (Pcs) has been found to be more favourable for more practical applications [5,6]. Phthalocyanines are synthetic pigments with innate optical and electronic properties that have enabled their usage in the design of sensors, optics and therapeutics amongst others [7–11].

This work explores the combination of Pcs and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) through both covalent and non-covalent interactions for the electrocatalytic oxidation of nitrite. Although there are reports on Pc-GQDs hybrid systems for electrocatalysis [12–14], the scope for further research remains in place as there are variations in both Pc modification and GQDs that can be applied in order to establish the most suitable combination. In this work we compare the effect of symmetry/asymmetry with the effect of a localized push-pull effect of the substituent on the Pc ring, in the presence of NGQDs.

Asymmetric Pcs containing substituents composed of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups have been shown to produce a push-pull effect, which is beneficial in electrocatalysis [14]. Substitution on the alpha or beta positions of the phthalocyanine derived from alcohol-based substituents often diminishes this effect due to the C-O-C bond and for this reason, acetaminophen (paracetamol) was selected as the dominant substituent in the synthesis of an A3B-type Pc. The acetaminophen substituent was employed as it can bring forth a push-pull effect on its own through resonance [15,16]. The aforementioned substituent was paired with an amine-based substituent to enable conjugation to the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) through the formation of an amide bond. In addition to conjugation through covalent means, the asymmetric complex alongside its symmetric counterpart (obtained as a side-product in the purification process) were π-stacked to the NGQDs (non-covalent interactions). The purpose of adopting this trajectory in the electrochemical sensing of nitrite is to illustrate the many ways in which a reduction in symmetry of Pcs can be leveraged to create novel molecular complexes and ultimately, create highly efficient electrocatalysts with a high sensitivity, selectivity and stability needed for practical use. Although legal in the pharmaceutical and chemical industries, above a certain level, nitrite can reduce the amount of oxygen carried by blood as well as cause stomach cancer by forming N-nitrosamines in the stomach, rendering it fatal hence the constant search for sensors that can detect it at trace levels [17].

1,8-Diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU), cobalt (II) chloride anhydrous (CoCl2), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Type I water from ELGA LabWater was used to prepare the aqueous solutions. 4-(4-Aminophenoxy)phthalonitrile (I) [18], and N-(4-(3,4-dicyanophenoxy)phenyl) acetamide (II) [19] were synthesized as reported in previous works. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were synthesized from citric acid and urea as a source of nitrogen (see Scheme S1 in the supporting information) as reported in literature [20]. All other reagents and solvents including N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were of analytical grade and were used without additional purification.

The mass spectral data was obtained from a Bruker AutoFLEXIII smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as the matrix. The absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV–Vis 2550 spectrophotometer. Infra-red spectra were collected using a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with a platinum universal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) sampling accessory. Elemental analysis was performed using a Vario-Elementar Microcube ELIII. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectral analyses were conducted using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer. Raman spectra were obtained from a Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer fitted with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and a liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector. The hydrodynamic sizes of the CoPc-NGQDs composites were determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

All electrochemical experiments with the exception of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis were conducted on a Bioanalytical Systems 100W Electrochemical Analyzer, using a three-electrode setup, where the glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm diameter) served as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and a silver/silver chloride electrode (in 3.0 M KCl) as the reference electrode. EIS experiments were carried out using an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302 N model). Solutions were purged with argon gas with the argon atmosphere being retained throughout the duration of the experiments. The applied potential was set at 0.24 V, at an amplitude of 1.0 × 10−5 ARMS with the frequency ranging from 0.1 to 105 Hz. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

4-(4-Aminophenoxy)phthalonitrile (I) (0.14 g, 0.58 mmol), N-(4-(3,4-dicyanophenoxy)phenyl) acetamide (II) (0.40 g, 1.44 mmol) and CoCl2 (0.094 g, 0.72 mmol) were added to dry N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (15 mL) containing DBU (0.5 mL). The mixture was refluxed over a period of 24 h in an argon atmosphere. Upon completion, the product was cooled to room temperature after which the crude sample was precipitated using methanol and water (v/v 1:1) and then dried in an oven at 60 °C. The desired compound was obtained as the first fraction using THF as the mobile phase and Si60 as the stationary phase. Yield: 58 mg, 8.66% (w/w); UV/vis (DMF): λ max/nm (log Ɛ): 665 (4.86), 604 (4.37), 326(4.80). IR [(KBr), ν max/cm-1]: 740 (aromatic C-Hstr), 1089 (C-O-C), 1225 (C-N), 1462 (C=C), 1501 (C-H), 1662 (C=O), 2865, 2922 (aliphatic C-Hstr), 3257 (N-H). Anal. calc. for C62H42CoN12O7•2H2O: C, 64.1; H, 3.99; N, 14.5. Found: C, 64.0; H, 3.56; N, 13,3. MS (MALDI-TOF) (m/z): calc.: 1126.27 amu; found: 1126.14 amu [M]+.

Complex 2 was obtained as a one of the side-products from the synthesis of complex 1. It was eluted as the second fraction using THF as the mobile phase and Si60 as the stationary phase. Yield: 135 mg, 8.02% (w/w); UV/vis (DMF): λ max/nm (log Ɛ): 672 (4.66), 611 (4.16), 328 (4.52). IR [(KBr), ν max/cm−1]: 750 (aromatic C-Hstr), 1095 (C-O-C), 1237 (C-N), 1462 (C=C), 1505 (C-H), 1666 (C=O), 2916 (aliphatic C-Hstr). Anal. calc. for C64H44CoN12O8•2H2O: C, 63.8; H, 4.02; N, 14.0. Found: C, 63.4; H, 3.78; N, 14.0. MS (MALDI-TOF) (m/z): calc.: 1167.27 amu; found: 1170.09 amu [M+3H]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of phthalocyanines to NGQDs, Scheme 2

ce:section.ce:para: The approach undertaken in creating the CoPc-NGQDs composites through π-π stacking was adopted from a previous study involving similar CoPcs [14]. Briefly, for each CoPc derivative, 3.2 mg (2.84 × 10−6 mol for 1, and 2.74 × 10−6 mol for 2) was dissolved in DMF (1 mL), whilst separately, 3.2 mg of the NGQDs were dissolved in 1 mL of water. Subsequently, the two solutions were mixed together and sonicated at room temperature for a period of 4 h followed by constant stirring at room temperature for 5 days. Ethanol was used for precipitating and washing (twice) the conjugates which were then dried under vacuum. These conjugates from this point forward are referred to as 1πNGQDs and 2πNGQDs, where the number refers to the CoPc complex, the “π” symbol denoting π-π stacking of the CoPc and NGQDs.

Much like the non-covalent method of conjugation, the method of conjugating the NGQDs to complex 1 through covalent interactions was also adopted from the same study [14]. An amide bond was formed using the primary amine (NH2) from complex 1 and the carboxylic acid groups (COOH) from the NGQDs. Briefly, complex 1 (4.0 mg, 3.56 × 10−6 mol) was dissolved in DMF (1 mL), and separately, NGQDs (4.0 mg) were dissolved in water (2.0 mL). To the NGQDs solution, the coupling agents NHS (0.10 g, 8.69 × 10−4 mol) and EDC (0.10 g, 6.44 × 10−4 mol) were added and and the mixture was sonicated until everything was dissolved. To the NGQDs mixture, complex 1 (in DMF) was added and the resultant mixture was sonicated further for an additional 4 h after which it was left to stir at room temperature for period of 48 h. Following that, the conjugate was precipitated with ethanol, and washed twice with ethanol before drying under vacuum. The covalently linked conjugate is, from this point forward, referred to as 1@NGQDs.

Electrode modification was carried out using the drop-and-dry method. Prior to modification, the electrode surface was cleaned by polishing on a SiC-emery paper in a slurry of alumina nanopowder (Sigma-Aldrich) and rinsed with Millipore water repeatedly. For the CoPcs alone, from a solution of 0.14 mM in DMF, 10 μL was dropped onto the electrode surface and dried in an oven overnight at 60 °C. With respect to the conjugates, 1.0 mg of the conjugates (or NGQDs alone) were dissolved in 1.0 mL of DMF (1.0 mL of water for the NGQDs), followed by electrode modification using the same volume and drying protocol explained for the CoPcs. The electrodes fabricated are from hereon, referred to as GCE-1, GCE-2 and GCE-NGQDs for single entities, and GCE-1πNGQDs, GCE-1@NGQDs and GCE-2πNGQDs for the corresponding π- and covalently formed conjugates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of complexes 1, 2 and their conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the syntheses of the CoPc derivatives. The synthesis of asymmetric Pcs (A3B) is often marred by the large probability of obtaining multiple side-products when starting from two phthalonitriles. And while there have been numerous reports on A3B-type Pcs, a gap still remains in the optimization process as the yield of these compounds not only depends on the stochiometric ratios between the phthalonitriles but also on the substituent each phthalonitrile possesses [14,21]. As a result, with each new A3B Pc synthesized, a different ratio is adopted to study any possible trends that may come about in the experimental process. For complex 1, the ratio between the two phthalonitriles was 1:2.5 with the yield recorded not indicating any improvement through lowering the ratio between the phthalonitriles. The synthesis of complex 1 was accompanied by the formation of the tetra-substituted paracetamol CoPc (complex 2) which was also met with a significantly low yield. It is important to note that the yields presented are not a true reflection of the efficiency of varying the ratios as the percentages calculated are not inclusive of the mixtures that could not be separated to a satisfactory level during the purification process. Both fractions for complexes 1 and 2, were obtaining using THF as the eluting solvent. The separation was achieved due to different solubilities of the complexes in THF.

In characterizing the synthesized CoPcs, several spectroscopic techniques were employed in confirming that the desired compounds had been obtained and purified successfully. For complex 1, a mass of 1126.14 amu was obtained from the mass spectral analysis which corresponds with the anticipated mass without the loss or addition of other ions or atoms (Fig. S1). A mass of 1170.09 amu was recorded for complex 2 which corresponds to the expected mass with the addition of three protons. Elemental analyses was also carried out with the results being in support of the predicted values in terms of the elemental composition for carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen respectively. The percentages obtained from the elemental analyses suggest that the complexes are hydrates which has been reported to be the case for some Pcs [22].

The ease with which Pcs and NGQDs were merged into singular nanocomposites through π-π stacking was facilitated by the structural makeup of the NGQDs which consists of planar sheets dominated by a network of sp2 carbons (Scheme 2A). Complex 1 was also linked to the NGQDs through a covalent bond (an amide bond). Using high resolution XPS spectra for C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, the presence of –NH–, -COOH, and -OH groups on the surface of NGQDs has been established [23], hence their representation in Scheme 2 (See also Scheme S1 in Supporting Information).

The amide bond was achieved through the activation of the carboxylic acid terminals on the NGQDs with EDC and NHS after which, the NH2 terminal from complex 1 was able to form a new bond with the NGQDs (Scheme 2B). The time applied for the covalent linking was shorter than that π-π stacking to reduce the likelihood of having a composite that is dominated largely by π-π interactions and not the intended, covalent bond, however, π-π interactions are also a possibility.

The absorption spectra of complex 1 in various organic solvents is presented in Fig. 1 A. In DMF, complex 1 showed a Q band absorption at 665 nm, (Fig. 1A), Table 1 . In the other solvents, THF and DMSO, the spectral changes are of an insignificant magnitude with the observed changes being attributed largely to the differences in the polarity of the solvents (Fig. 1A).

With respect to the conjugates, the absorption spectra were marked by some spectral shifts of the Q bands relative to the complexes on their own (Fig. 1B for complex 1 and Fig. S2 for complex 2). In each case, for 1πNGQDs and 1@NGQDs, a red shift in the Q bands was observed compared to complex 1, whilst for 2πNGQDs, a blue shift in the Q band was observed. Red shifts in porphyrin-graphene oxide nanoconjugates have been reported to be caused by molecular flattening [24]. The blue shift in Fig. S2 is an indication of an interaction of Pcs with the surface of the NGQDs. Similar blue shifts upon conjugation of semi-conductor quantum dots to porphyrins have been reported and were attributed to surface plasma changes as the porphyrin is deposited on the surface of the QDs [25]. For all conjugates, the Q band retains its characteristic trait of an intense absorption which suggests that conjugation does not have an adverse influence on the structural integrity of the Pc molecule. The increase in absorbance below 400 nm is due to the absorption by NGQDs in Fig. 1B, Fig. S2.

Consequently, the UV–Vis spectra were applied in determining the loading of Pc onto the NGQDs using a method reported in the literature [26], through monitoring the absorbance of the Q band of the Pc before and after conjugation. The loading for each conjugate was determined with the results presented in Table 1, where it can be seen that complex 2 in 2πNGQDs has a better loading than its asymmetric counterparts in 1πNGQDs and 1@NGQDs. The loading for complex 1 was lower in the covalently linked conjugate than it was for the π-stacked conjugate which may be due to the spatial orientation of the structures in question.

The infrared (IR) spectra of the NGQDs, alongside that of complex 1 and the covalently linked conjugated are displayed in Fig. 2 . An in-depth analysis of the NGQDs has been provided in our previous works [14]. Functional groups from the paracetamol substituent for complex 1 are observed at 2865 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 for the aliphatic carbons (Fig. 2).

Due to the similarities in neighbouring atoms, the N-H stretch frequency observed at 3257 cm−1 is assigned to either that of the amine group (from the phenoxy substituent) or that present in the paracetamol-based substituent (Fig. 2). Other functional groups arising from the Pc's main framework (with aromatic-based functional groups possibly overlapping with the phenyl rings in the substituents) are due to stretching/vibrational frequencies at 680 cm−1 (aromatic C-Hstr), 1225 cm−1 (C-N) and 1462 cm−1 (C=C) respectively (Fig. 2). The absence of the C≡N vibrations of the phthalonitriles near 2230 cm−1 confirms cyclization to form the phthalocyanines (Fig. S3).

The FT-IR spectrum associated with the covalently linked conjugate, 1@NGQDs, has traits of both components with subtle differences, suggesting an enmeshment of the two (Fig. 2). Of interest in this case, are stretching/vibrational frequencies associated with the amide group. Looking at 1@NGQDs, tentative assignments marked by shifts in stretching or vibrational modes are made where the stretching frequency at 3279 cm−1 is assigned to an N-H amide-based stretch with greater transmittance suggesting the presence/creation of more amide bonds. A stretch of medium intensity at 1113 cm−1 is ascribed to the C-O stretch (in C-O-C), a slight shift towards a higher frequency relative to when complex 1 is on its own at 1089 cm−1 (Fig. 2). In addition to all the observations reported above, an intense carbonyl stretch makes its appearance at 1630 cm−1 (Fig. 2).

Due to the fact that complex 1 has amide bonds on its own which stem from the acetaminophen substituent, an additional technique was employed to verify the formation of the amide bond between complex 1 and the NGQDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) are shown in Fig. 3 (% atomic compositions shown in Table S1) which include wide scans of complex 1 and the conjugate, 1@NGQDs, as well as the deconvoluted spectra for the O 1s region (XPS wide scan and O 1s high resolution spectra for the NGQDs are shown in Fig. S4). The general observation that can be made regarding the wide scans is that one can roughly estimate the presence or quantity of a certain element based on the intensities each element registers. Prior to conjugation, complex 1 has a lower atomic concentration of oxygen and nitrogen than the NGQDs. Following conjugation, the atomic concentration of nitrogen in the conjugate is higher than that observed in complex 1 and the NGQDs separately. Regarding the atomic concentration of oxygen, the value observed in the conjugate is lower than that in the NGQDs alone as the formation of an amide bond is accompanied by the loss of the -OH group on the carboxylic acid terminals (Table S1).

The O 1s region in complex 1 was deconvoluted to two bands, where the first one at a lower binding energy of 529.5 eV (5007 cps) was assigned to the ether group (C-O-C) while the second one, at a higher binding energy of 530.6 eV (5089 cps), was assigned to the carbonyl oxygen (N-(C=O)) (Fig. 3C). Three bands, similar to Shi and colleagues [27], were observed in the analysis of the O 1s spectrum associated with the NGQDs (Fig. S4B). Peak binding energies were observed at 530.4 eV (18660 cps), 531.1 eV (16449 cps) and 532.1 eV (9552 cps) which were assigned to C-O, C=O, (O=C)-O) groups, respectively (Fig. S4B). The covalently formed conjugate, 1@NGQDs, yielded five bands upon deconvolution with assignments being made as follows: 528.6 eV (4086 cps) for C-O-C, 529.3 eV (7892 cps) for N-(C=O), 530.0 eV (9965 cps) for C-O, 531.0 eV (6798 cps) for C=O and 532.4 eV (3419 cps for (O=C)-O, (Fig. 3D). What is of importance in this instance is the increase in the intensity of the band associated with the amide carbonyl N-(C=O) from 5089 cps for complex 1 alone to 7892 cps for 1@NGQDs. There was a decrease in the intensity of the band assigned to the (O=C)-O group in the carboxylic acid of NGQDs (from 9552 cps to 3419 cps of the conjugate), this is due to the loss of OH upon the formation of the amide bond. Overall, there was a general decline in all the other bands due to a decrease in the original starting material.

An assessment of the order/disorder of the graphene network that forms the NGQDs was performed (using Raman spectroscopy) to better understand the effects that conjugation has on the graphene matrix. Shifts in both the D and G bands were observed (Fig. 4 ). The Raman spectral shifts towards either the blue or red regions are attributed to the differences in the forces (compressive versus tensile) that act on the graphene framework [28,29]. Compressive forces are those that result in pushing atoms towards each other while tensile forces stretch bonds, resulting in atoms being pulled further from each other. The ID/IG ratios along with the Raman shifts in both the D and G bands are ascribed to the interactions between the NGQDs and complexes (1 and 2) after conjugation. The effects of conjugation of carbon nanomaterials are often quantified by the ID/IG ratio where a low ID/IG ratio is often associated with a low level of disorder whilst a high ID/IG ratio corresponds to a high level of disorder in the graphene network. The ID/IG ratios observed were greater than that of the NGQDs alone. The ID/IG ratios presented in Table 1 are as follows: 0.16, 0.39, 0.43 and 0.31 for the NGQDs, 1πNGQDs, 1@NGQDs and 2πNGQDs respectively. Lower ID/IG values were recorded for the π-stacked conjugates relative to the composite derived from covalent linking. This may be due to the Pc and their substituents assuming spatial positions that cause minimal strain to the graphene matrix, unlike the covalently-formed nanocomposite which may result in the occurrence of pulling forces (tensile forces) caused by the formation of new bonds between the NGQDs and the Pc.

Using dynamic light-scattering (DLS), the size of the NGQDs following conjugation to the complexes was shown to have increased from 1.7 nm to between 26.9 nm and 37.1 nm (Table 1, Fig. S5). DLS size is affected by dispersing agent resulting in large hydrodynamic diameter [30]. In addition to influences from the dispersant, an enhancement in the electron density after conjugation may also account for the sizes observed. Taking these factors into consideration, these sizes may not necessarily reflect the actual size of a single nanocomposite as these elements promote the formation of clusters (conjugates may be smaller). These clusters, often referred to as aggregates, are a common occurrence in Pc-GQDs or other Pc-nanoparticle composites [31,32].

The lowest ΔEp value was recorded for the bare GCE (71 mV), which is not only synonymous with desirable electron-transporting abilities but is also often associated with a clean electrode surface. Regarding the complexes as single entities, GCE-1 presented a ΔEp value of 109 mV whilst a value of 275 mV was recorded for GCE-2, suggesting that the asymmetric CoPc is a better conductor of electrons in comparison to its symmetric analogue. A value of 187 mV recorded for the NGQDs alone inferred that the carbon-based materials, in this particular case, are more favourable than the symmetric Pc, complex 2. Although it appeared that the asymmetric Pc possesses better electron-transfer properties in its isolated form, this observation was not upheld when the complexes are linked to the NGQDs as GCE-1πNGQDs has a larger ΔEp value than GCE-2πNGQDs showing no advantage of asymmetry in the presence of NGQDs.

Looking at the π-stacked and covalently linked conjugates derived from complex 1, GCE-1πNGQDs and GCE-1@NGQDs, the lower ΔEp value was recorded for the covalently linked conjugate (446 mV), showing the advantage of covalent linking. Overall, there was no definitive trend observed in comparing the CoPcs alone to their conjugates as GCE-1 had the lowest ΔEp value in comparison to its conjugates, GCE-1πNGQDs and GCE-1@NGQDs while GCE-2 had the higher ΔEp value relative to its corresponding conjugate, GCE-2πNGQDs. A larger peak-to-peak separation indicates that the rate of electron transfer has been reduced and has been observed for electrodes modified with graphene materials [33]. According to the literature, electrochemically active species that are adsorbed onto the electrode often produce such cyclic voltammograms with their appearance being attributed to reasons such as slow electron-transfer processes, non-ideal/deviant performance from the adsorbed materials as well as potential discrepancies in the adsorption sites which in turn may influence the value of the redox couple, resulting in a deviation from what may be considered the standard value(s) [34]. The redox peaks near −300 mV could be due to CoII/CoI processes of the MPc [35].

Regarding EIS, a large RCT value, often distinguished by a Nyquist plot bearing a large semi-circle, is associated with poor or limited electron-transferring abilities [36]. From Fig. 6 and the RCT values recorded in Table 2, deductions were made as follows:
•
A low RCT value of 0.18 kΩ was registered for the bare GCE, showing good conductivity.
•
Much like the ΔEp values, GCE-1 produced the better value of 19.1 kΩ while a value of 30.4 kΩ was recorded for GCE-2.
•
Relative to the complexes alone, the NGQDs yielded the lowest RCT value (4.51 kΩ) which may be attributed to the similarities in the arrangement of atoms between the bare GCE and the NGQDs.
•
An increment in the RCT values was observed for the conjugates associated with the asymmetric CoPc, GCE-1πNGQDs (28.2 kΩ) and GCE-1@NGQDs (22.0 kΩ) compared to complex 1 alone, whilst the opposite was observed for the conjugate derived from the tetra-substituted CoPc, where a decrease for GCE-2πNGQDs (19.4 kΩ) was observed compared to complex 2 (30.4 kΩ) alone. The observed changes follow the trends in ΔEp values.
•
With a low RCT value being an indicator of high-electron transferring abilities, based on the results obtained, the modified electrodes were ranked as follows: NGQDs > GCE-1 > GCE-2πNGQDs > GCE-1@NGQDs > GCE-1πNGQDs > GCE-2.

Quite often, a large ΔEp value is accompanied by a large RCT value as both are predictors of charge transferring capabilities however [36], this trend was not necessarily linear in the electrodes presented. The manner in which the EIS results were in agreement with the ΔEp values was in an isolated form where the results are agreeable in terms of the complexes on their own and their corresponding conjugates, and not taking into consideration all probes (Table 2). Looking at the summary of these observations in Table 2, it is evident that while the two techniques are considered complementary to one another, the difference in their sensitivities is useful in unmasking factors which may or may not contribute to the discrepancies observed. These differences may be ascribed to the proportions of the Pc to the NGQDs in the nanocomposite or simply non-ideal behaviour. The corresponding Bode plots (Fig. S6), show that the phase angle values for all electrode surfaces studied in this work are less than the ideal 90° for an ideal capacitor [37].

Chronocoulometric experiments were carried out in the redox probe to enable the determination of the effective area of the electrode. In order to oxidize Fe(CN)6 3− (aq) to Fe(CN)6 4− (aq), the potential was stepped from 0.0 V to 1.0 V which produced a chronocoulometric graph (Fig. S7A, GCE-1 as an example). This figure (Fig. S7A) was converted to a charge (C) vs time1/2 (s) graph (Fig. S7B) and further used to determine the surface area of the electrode from the integrated Cottrell equation (Eq. (1)) [38]:

In which the slope is equivalent 2nFAD

In combination with the area obtained from using Eq. (1), the effective surface coverage was calculated from equation (2) (Eq. (2)) as expressed in previous reports [38]:
(2)

where
Γ
represents the surface coverage, Q the electrical charge integrated from the area assigned to the CoII/III process, from the linear sweep voltammetry (Fig. S8), n the number of electrons involved (n = 1), F being Faraday's constant and A, the effective area (calculated using Eq. (1)). The peaks labelled I in Fig. 7 A and B may be due to CoIIPc−2/CoIPc−2 in CoPc containing electrodes [35]. The peaks labelled II are ascribed to CoIIIPc−2/CoIIPc−2 processes while III are due to ring-based processes [35]. These peak assignments also confirm the presence of our chosen modifiers at the electrode surface. Γ values are higher than the 1 × 10−10 mol/cm2 ascribed to Pcs assuming a monolayer on the electrode surface where the Pc molecules are in a flat position [40]. The values presented in Table 2 exceed what is often reported with values ranging between 6.08 × 10−8 mol/cm2 and 8.40 × 10−7 mol/cm2. These values, although slightly higher than what has been reported [41], are said to correspond to electrode surfaces in which the film on the electrode surface is not a monolayer but rather a multi-layered film [41,42], which may be the result of combining the CoPcs with the carbon-based nanomaterials, the NGQDs. The Γ values are higher for the CoPcs alone than their conjugates which may imply that the inclusion of the NGQDs promote a multi-layered arrangement of the composites on the electrode surface (which is plausible as π-stacking results in such structures). The effective area for GCE-NGQDs was not calculated as there was no reduction peak using the LSV to enable determination of the charge through integration.

The electrochemical detection of nitrite was performed using various techniques including cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) as well as chronoamperometry. Using voltammetric techniques (Fig. 7C and D), the sensing of nitrite can be carried out through reduction, oxidation or both. In this instance, detection was carried out through oxidation alone as the reduction of molecular oxygen falls within the same potential window as nitrite reduction [43].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a favourable technique in assessing the fundamental aspects of a modified electrode surface, a reduction in the oxidation potential and an increase in the catalytic currents are evidence for good electrocatalytic activity. From the results in Table 2 (Fig. 7C and D), regarding the complexes on their own, GCE-1 and GCE-2, the differences between the two in terms of the oxidation potential was of a negligible magnitude. The same observation was made for the NGQDs in relation to the Pcs (Table 2). Regarding complex 1 and its corresponding conjugates, the composite derived from non-covalent interactions produced the most favourable result with a value of 941 mV (GCE-1πNGQDs). This was followed by GCE-1 and GCE-1@NGQDs with values of 971 mV and 1018 mV, respectively. For the symmetric complex, GCE-2πNGQDs produced the better oxidation potential in comparison to GCE-2 (lower). Overall, GCE-2πNGQDs produced the most favourable oxidation potential as it was the lowest from the all probes studied (GCE-2πNGQDs < GCE-1πNGQDs < GCE-2 < GCE-NGQDs < GCE-1 < GCE-1@NGQDs). With the decrease in the oxidation potential only being observed in the π-π stacked conjugates, it is evident that the quantity of the Pc in the conjugate has a significant influence on the movement of electrons on the electrode. This may be the result of either the symmetry of the complexes, the substituents or a combination of both. Complex 1 has one of the acetaminophen substituents replaced by the NH2-bearing substituent which may be responsible for the reduced loading for complex 1 in comparison to complex 2. The bare GCE was marked by an ill-defined cyclic voltammogram, Fig. S9.

With regards to the current, GCE-2 produced a better signal (higher) compared to GCE-1 (57.1 μA > 45.2 μA). For complex 1 and the conjugates; GCE-1 produced the highest peak catalytic current followed by GCE-1@NGQDs and then GCE-1πNGQDs (GCE-1 > GCE-1@NGQDs > GCE-1πNGQDs) (Table 2). GCE-2 produced the highest current across all probes studied (GCE-2 > GCE-2πNGQDs > GCE-1 > GCE-NQGDs∼GCE-1@NGQDs > GCE-1πNGQDs) (Table 2). While it has been shown that the non-covalently linked composites promote better electron transfer as marked by an increment in the current [13], the opposite was observed in this work and this may be explained by the kinetic processes that occur on the electrode surface.

The Tafel slope values, derived from a Tafel plot (Fig. S10B, GCE-2πNGQDs shown as an example), are presented in Table 3 . According to the literature, an ideal Tafel slope lies between 60 and 120 mV/decade with values exceeding 120 mV/decade having no meaning in the kinetic sense [44,45]. In addition to that, a value of 120 mV/decade is often observed in the event that the rate-determining step is the first electron transfer step while that of 60 mV/decade is associated with a rapid electron transfer process followed by a steady chemical step [46,47]. The Tafel slopes observed, derived in a similar manner from a report by Mohammadizadeh and colleagues [48], ranged from 71.3 to 128 mV/decade (Table 3). Apart from the increment in the Tafel values being observed for the nanohybrids, there is no definitive trend in the results presented (Table 3). An assumption based on what has been previously described regarding nitrogen-containing catalysts is that there may be adsorption on the electrode surface [49]. While the Tafel values were significantly lower, the presence of nitrogen in the molybdenum lattice was reported to foster kinetics driven by proton adsorption [49]. And due to the nature of the materials used in fabricating the electrodes reported in this study, similar inferences pertaining to the results observed can be made (hence low peak currents were observed in the cyclic voltammetric experiments (Fig. 7C and 7D)).

The transfer coefficient was determined according to equation (3) [47]:
(3)
α
=
47.7
E p
−
E
p / 2
m V
where α is the transfer coefficient, Ep the peak oxidation potential, and Ep/2, the potential where the current is half the value it is at its peak oxidation. A transfer coefficient of 0.5 indicates that from the transition state, there is an equal chance of reactants or products being formed at equilibrium; a value of 1 corresponds primarily to product formation whilst a value of zero or close to zero is associated with reactants being more favourable at equilibrium [50]. Using eq. (3), the α values obtained where all close to zero ranging from 0.22 to 0.34 (Table 3). These values results suggest adsorption of the reactants onto the electrode surface were made. Nonetheless, the mechanism for nitrite was determined based on what has been previously reported regarding the oxidation of nitrite using CoPcs, where the nitrite ion coordinates to the central metal [51]. As such, the mechanism under which nitrite is oxidized is a 3-step, 1 electron mechanism which proceeds as follows:
(4) Co(II)Pc + NO2 − → [(NO2 −)Co(II)Pc]-
(5) [(NO2 −)Co(II)Pc]- → (NO2 −)Co(III)Pc + e−
(6) (NO2 −)Co(III)Pc + H2O → Co(II)Pc + NO3 − + 2H+ + e−
Where Eq. (4) stems from how nitrite is reported to bind axially to the cobalt centre while Eq. (5) is put forth according to where the catalytic oxidation of nitrite is often observed relative to Co3+ [51].

The chronoamperometric analyses (GCE-1πNGQDs shown as an example in Fig. 8 A) was used to determine the limits of detection (LoD) as well as the catalytic rate constant values. In Fig. 8A, the chronoamperograms show a uniform increase in the current as the concentration of nitrite is increased. The calibration curve (inset in Fig. 8A) where the current is plotted against the concentration yielded a straight line where the gradient was equated to the sensitivity of the probe. Using a common approach to determining limit of detection (LoD) values, the 3δ/gradient method (where δ is the standard deviation in the buffer in the absence of the analyte), the lowest LoD value was recorded for GCE-2πNGQDs followed by GCE-NGQDs (Table 3). GCE-1πNGQDs showed a minor improvement in the LoD compared to complex 1 alone while GCE-1@NGQDs performed poorly relative to complex 1 alone (GCE-1), showing the disadvantage of covalent linking (as it may result in a reduced loading of the Pc within the conjugate). On its own, complex 1 (GCE-1) performs better than 2 (GCE-2) in terms of the LoD but in the presence of NGQDs, GCE-2πNGQDs performs better than GCE-1πNGQDs (Table 3). Although the cyclic voltammetric results do not entirely support the use of conjugates in the electrocatalysis of nitrite (Table 2), the use of a more sensitive technique has demonstrated the benefits of such systems (Pc-NGQDs hybrids) with a significant reduction in the LoD for complex 2 in GCE-2πNGQDs. And while this notion cannot be extended to the asymmetric complex (complex 1), this does not necessarily imply that the NGQDs are incapable of improving the electrocatalytic abilities of complex 1. The results observed may be largely due to complex 1 exhibiting a poorer loading in both the π-stacked and covalently formed conjugate (hence the results obtained).

Plots of the ratios between the current in the presence of the analyte (I cat ) and in the absence of the analyte (I buf ) against the square root of time (s) produced linear graphs with varying slopes as a result of the varying concentrations (Fig. 8B). Eq. (7)) was employed for the determination of the catalytic rate constant [52]:

cat , the catalytic rate constant. The catalytic rate constants were determined from the slopes of the plots of the concentration of nitrite versus the squared slopes from Fig. 8B (Fig. 8C). The linear plots are represented by Eqs. (8a)-(8f)
(8a) GCE-NGQDs – y = 102.45[NO2 −] – 19.742, R2 = 0.983
(8b) GCE-1 – y = 2.3549[NO2 −] – 0.3862, R2 = 0.9879
(8c) GCE-1πNGQDs – y = 23.428[NO2 −] – 2.3209, R2 = 0.9894
(8d) GCE-1@NGQDs – y = 0.9683[NO2 −] - 0.1216, R2 = 0.9914

The lowest catalytic rate was recorded for GCE-NGQDs while GCE-1 produced the highest catalytic rate constant followed by GCE-1@NGQDs. The most notable observation that was made in the catalytic rate constants was that the inclusion of the NGQDs resulted in a lowering of the values (complexes on their own versus complexes within a conjugate) with the margins thereof being variable, hence the absence of a definitive trend. Furthermore, the kcat values reported in this study remained more favourable than that of the NGQDs on their own (GCE-NGQDs) (Table 3).

The stability of the electrodes was studied through cyclic voltammetry where the electrodes were subjected to multiple continuous scans at a fixed concentration of nitrite. An unstable electrode is often characterized by its failure to retain the peak current and oxidation potential at more or less the same positions or by the complete disappearance of the peak catalytic current after several successive scans. Of the probes presented in this study, based on the extent to which the current changes from the first scan to the last scan, GCE-1πNGQDs was found to present the least stability as it registered the lowest change (decline of 7% after 20 scans) while GCE-2 was the most stable with a current increment of 33% (Fig. 9 ). The stability of all the probes, from most stable to least stable was ranked based on the change in the peak current as follows: GCE-2 > GCE-1 > GCE-1@NGQDs > GCE-2πNGQDs < GCE-1πNGQDs.

Due to the fact that GCE-2πNGQDs registered the lowest LoD value, a further assessment on its stability was undertaken. Through recording cyclic voltammograms at a fixed concentration of 1.0 mM, following storage in a refrigerator at 4 °C for a period of 7 days, the peak catalytic current was found to have decreased by 18%, and while it is a significant decline, the usability of the probe remains intact (Fig. S11).

The use of CoPcs and Pc-NGQDs nanocomposites as sensors towards the electrochemical detection of nitrite has been demonstrated. While the general expectation was that the inclusion of NGQDs would be beneficial for all the CoPcs reported, this was not always the case. This was primarily due to the loading or quantity of the Pc molecule within the conjugate itself. The observations made are a clear indication that the structural differences in Pcs, no matter how subtle, have a significant influence on their functionality, more so their interactions with other nanomaterials (hence there is a better loading for complex 2 than there is for complex 1). In terms of the LoD values, the asymmetric CoPc (complex 1, GCE-1) as a single entity performs better than its symmetric counterpart, complex 2 (GCE-2). In the presence of the NGQDs as nanocomposites, complex 2 is rendered the superior probe with GCE-2πNGQDs producing the lowest LoD with a value of 0.70 μM. Overall, the presented electrodes show great potential as electrochemical sensors for nitrite and the design thereof may be useful in informing how other electrocatalysts for other analytes are designed.
Reitumetse Nkhahle: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
